صلاة pl. صلوات
In Islam, صلاة means performing the salat, with the standing, kneeling, prostration, and performing ablution before. Supplication to God (asking him for stuff), on the other hand, is called دعاء. In Christianity however, both general prayer and asking him for stuff is called صلاة.

Originally: منتهى
ى becomes ا when a suffix is added.

translation: the Arabian Gulf, Persian Gulf, Gulf (states)
info:
Literally: the Gulf
This is a region that includes the Arab states of Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates. Iraq and Iran are technically part of the Gulf region as well (in a geographic sense), but for historical and political reasons, they're usually excluded. Neither Iraq nor Iran is part of the GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council).

Literally: crown of (someone's) head
It's used when one "values" their loved one so much that they're like a crown above their head. There's a proverb that says: الصحة تاج على رؤوس الأصحاء (health is a crown above the heads of the healthy), which means that one's health is highly valued.

لا حول الله is a shortened version of لا حَوْلَ ولا قُوَّةَ إلا بِاللهِ (there's no power and no strength save in God). Though لا حول الله remains commonly used, some religious leaders warn against using it not only because it's wrong, but because it could suggest that God doesn't have strength at all (لا حول الله literally translates as "no power of God")

info: Jihad is an Arabic word which literally means striving or struggling, especially with a praiseworthy aim. In an Islamic context, it can refer to almost any effort to make personal and social life conform with God's guidance, such as struggle against one's evil inclinations, proselytizing, or efforts toward the moral betterment of the ummah, though it is most frequently associated with war. (Wikipedia)

info: The She-Camel of God (Arabic: نَـاقـة الله‎) in Islam was a miraculous female camel sent by God to the people of Thamud in Al-Hijr, after they demanded a miracle from the prophet Saleh. Thamūd is mentioned twenty-three times in the Quran as part of a moralistic lesson about God's destruction of sinful nations, a central motif in the Quran. (Wikipedia)

تلج (or ثلج in MSA) means ice or snow. The lack of distinction is said to originate from the fact that the Arabs of yore, a desert people, rarely saw either (in contrast with Alaska's eskimo people, who allegely have dozens of words for snow).

ضمير pl. ضمائر
الضَّمِيْر ‘the pronoun’ in Arabic is either overt or covert. The covert is called مُسْتَتِر, and it is neither written nor pronounced. The overt is called ظَاهِر, and it can be separate مُنْفَصِل or attached مُتَّصِل.
MSA: ربع روب is used most commonly for telling time.

translation: The Most Exalted (one of the 99 names of God in Islam)
info: Names of God in Islam are 99 names that each contain attributes of God in Islam, which are implied by the respective names. These names usually denote his praise, gratitude, commendation, glorification, magnification, perfect attributes, majestic qualities, and acts of wisdom, mercy, benefit, and justice from Allah, as believed by Muslims. (Wikipedia)

The Magnificent (one of the 99 names of God in Islam)
info: Names of God in Islam are 99 names that each contain attributes of God in Islam, which are implied by the respective names. These names usually denote his praise, gratitude, commendation, glorification, magnification, perfect attributes, majestic qualities, and acts of wisdom, mercy, benefit, and justice from Allah, as believed by Muslims. (Wikipedia)

info:
This verb is often used to describe a favorable inclination toward something. 
Examples:
بامِيْلْ لُه أَكْتَر I prefer him/it;
قَلْبِي بِيْميل لِيها I'm fond of her
[livingarabic.com]

This could be said of time, but it could also refer to missing something, such as an opportunity. "The subject and object are reversed compared to what they are in English. As in, the thing you are missing, in Arabic, is the thing passing you."
[livingarabic.com]

info: "Compare فهم، أفطن، استوعب، أدرك، وعى. of these words فهم gives the most basic simplest meaning to understanding, استوعب suggests more depth, أفطن implies that one has grasped the inner meaning, أدرك is similar to the english 'to realize', and وعى means to be conscious of something." [livingarabic]

[used to request or demand that something be brought, given, or handed over]
info:
Used as a stand-alone phrase (example: !هات "give it to me/us!"), with a direct object (example: هات الكوبية "give me/us the cup!"), or with proposition ل (example: هات لنا الكوبية/هات لي الكوبية "give me/us the cup!")
Possible translations:
get
give
bring
hand over

info:
This is known as لام التوكيد (literally: the lam of confirmation). It's used to provide emphasis.
Possible translations: 
truly
verily
certainly
surely

Usually precedes a verb.
Possible translation:
how!

translation: [indicates the termination of an action in the past]
info:
Precedes verb conjugated in past tense.
Possible translations:
have
has

translation: across, through, by way of, by means of

info:
Possible translations:
take care not (to)!
don't do!
beware of!
watch out for!
Must always be followed by a past tense verb, as it confirms actions in the past. It differs from قد by having a higher degree of emphasis and being somewhat stylistic.

translation: and then, and all of a sudden
info:
إذا الفُجائِيّة (the إذا of surprise) is usually followed by a nominal sentence or just a noun and is usually introduced by ب, The nominal sentence is usually in the present tense.
Common translations: 
lo and behold
(and) all of a sudden
when suddenly
and suddenly there was

Consists of:
ل = intensifying particle meaning: truly, verily, certainly, surely, indeed, really)
إن = if
In MSA, فجأة appears with tanween ً . In colloquial, it does not.

translation: and that's all, and no more, only
hence, therefore, for that reason, consequently

info:
Sometimes translated as "not."
Examples:
ُلست I'm not
َلست you (masc) are not
ِلست you (fem) are not
َليس he is not
ليسَت she is not
لَسنا we're not
لستوا you (pl) are not
ليسوا they're not

Possible translations:
take care not (to)!
don't do!
beware of!
watch out for!
